summary: My grandfather was a rancher down at Mounds, Oklahoma. It was there that he came up to see me the day I was born and gave me the nickname 'Johnnie.' Mounds was part of my family's roots and early life stories.

I attended the University of Tulsa, or TU as we call it, and when it came time to get my diploma, Dean Langenheim asked what name I wanted on it. I chose to have 'Joan' on the diploma, even though I usually go by Johnnie. TU was an important part of my education and early adulthood.

I met my good friend Mary Ann Hille through the First Presbyterian Church just a couple of years ago. It was also likely where my mother and father met, as it was the social meeting place for young people in Tulsa at the time. The church has been a meaningful part of my social and family history.

We are recording this interview at The Villages of Southern Hills rest home here in Tulsa. It's where I am living now at the age of ninety-eight and a half. This place has been my home in recent years.

I was born in Tulsa, Oklahoma, at 905 South Denver in 1915, right here in the city. My family lived here through the oil boom, and my grandfather became postmaster of Tulsa in the late 1890s. Tulsa has been central to my life and family history.

I was born in 1915, right in the middle of World War I, which had started in 1914 and lasted until 1918. Though I was just a baby, the war was a significant backdrop to the time of my birth. It was a turbulent period in history that shaped the world I grew up in.

In the year I was born, 1915, the United States House of Representatives rejected a proposal to give women the right to vote. Interestingly, my women folks at the time were not interested in voting, which I find amusing now. It was a significant political event during my early years.

The first stone of the Lincoln Memorial was placed in Washington DC the year I was born, 1915. I didn't know that until recently, but it was one of several important events happening alongside my birth. It adds a historical significance to the year I came into the world.

In 1915, the same year I was born, Babe Ruth hit his first career home run off Jack Worhop. It was one of the notable sports moments of that year, and it always amuses me to think of such events happening as I was starting my life.

I spent my summers at a place called Gypsy Camp, which belonged to my grandfather and his wife, right on the bank of the Illinois River. I lived every day in the river, in boats, on the tennis court, or riding horseback, and I believe that active outdoor life gave me a good start for longevity.

I was born at 905 South Denver in Tulsa, Oklahoma, in a little house my parents bought before they were married. They even had their wedding there, and we lived in that house until I was about seven. I have fond memories of the backyard swing my father built for me there.

My mother was Elizabeth J. McAllister, from an old Georgia family that came north after the Civil War. Some of her family settled in Tulsa and Sapulpa. She was part of a lineage with deep roots and stories tied to the South and the war.

My mother's family came from North Georgia, where my grandfather John McAllister grew up. They lived there during the Civil War, and my grandfather shared stories about Federal troops invading their home. Georgia is where much of my family's early history took place.
My grandfather John McAllister lived in Sapulpa, Oklahoma, as a retired minister and lived into his nineties. Sapulpa was part of our family's later years and a place connected to his ministry work.

My grandfather, John McAllister, was a rancher in Mounds and later became postmaster of Tulsa in the late 1890s. He was a Confederate soldier's son from North Georgia and shared many Civil War stories with me. He was the one who gave me the nickname 'Johnnie' when I was born.

During the Civil War, Federal troops came into my grandfather's home in North Georgia and tore up the bedding searching for valuables. My grandfather was only about five years old then and had to watch over the last mule to keep it safe. Those stories give me a vivid picture of the hardships my family endured.

My great-grandfather was a Methodist preacher in North Georgia after the Civil War. Despite his imprisonment as a Confederate soldier, he returned to his ministry and family life. The Methodist Church was an important part of our family's heritage.

My great-grandfather served as a soldier in the Confederate States Army during the Civil War. He was taken prisoner and endured starvation rations, even eating rats to survive. His experiences deeply affected our family history and stories.

After the war, my great-grandfather led a wagon train of thirteen families to settle around Springfield, Missouri. My grandmother's family also settled there after immigrating from Scotland. Springfield became a central place for our family in the late 19th century.

Along with Springfield, my great-grandfather and family settled around Republic, Missouri after migrating from Georgia. It was part of the new home they built after the hardships of the Civil War. Republic holds a place in our family's migration story.

Wesley Mastin McAllister was my great-grandfather who led the wagon train from North Georgia to Missouri after the Civil War. Born and raised in North Georgia, he later moved his family westward seeking a better life. His leadership and resilience shaped our family's history.

My grandmother Lizzie Gettis McAllister was born and raised in Buckie, Banffshire, Scotland. She was a skilled musician and came to America as a graduation gift from her family. Buckie was her hometown and the start of her journey to America.

Banffshire is the county in Scotland where my grandmother Lizzie was born and raised, specifically in the town of Buckie. It was a place of her early education and musical training before she traveled to America. Banffshire connects our family to Scottish roots.

My grandmother came from Scotland, specifically Buckie in Banffshire. She was a talented musician who came to America as a graduation gift from her family. Scotland is a proud part of our family's heritage and story.

My grandmother traveled to America by sailing ship that landed somewhere on the Great Lakes before continuing on to Colorado. That journey was part of her trip from Scotland to join relatives in the United States. The Great Lakes were a key point in her immigration route.

My grandparents were married in Leadville, Colorado, where my grandmother arrived after her journey from Scotland. I visited Leadville as a child and saw the remains of the building where they were married. Leadville marks an important chapter in their early married life.

The Panic of 1893 was a financial crisis that wiped out my grandfather's business in Springfield, Missouri. It was a tough time for the family, but it led to new opportunities as he was sent to Tulsa by the Dickerson Goodman Company. That event changed the course of our family's history.

After losing his business in the Panic of 1893, my grandfather got acquainted with Mr. Dickerson of the Dickerson Goodman Company. Mr. Dickerson sent him to Tulsa to open their business here, which he ran successfully before becoming postmaster. The company played a key role in our family's move to Tulsa.

My grandfather, John McAllister, was appointed postmaster of Tulsa in the late 1890s, though it was a political appointment and didn't last long. His portrait is still displayed at the post office among other postmasters. This role was a notable part of his career in Tulsa.

My father was Emmett Claude Coe from Parker's Landing, Pennsylvania. He moved to Tulsa with his family during the oil boom and worked on drilling wells with his brother Ross. Dad's background in the oil industry was a big part of our family's life in Tulsa.

My father came from Parker's Landing, Pennsylvania, where his family lived before moving to Tulsa. The oil boom in Tulsa attracted them here after they lost their business back east. Parker's Landing was the starting point for my father's journey west.

Ross Coe was my father's brother, and together they worked for oil companies around Tulsa after moving here. They even had a small company for a while, though it was dissolved early on due to disagreements. Ross was part of the family's oil industry involvement.

The oil boom in Tulsa was the reason my father's family moved here from Pennsylvania. My dad and uncle worked on drilling wells and were involved in the oil business during that exciting time. The boom shaped much of our family's life and opportunities in Tulsa.

The first school I attended was Horace Mann Junior High School at 10th and Boston in Tulsa. My mother thought it was too dangerous for me to ride a bicycle to school because of the busy streets. That school was the start of my formal education.

We lived across from J.E. Crosby's mansion when I was going to Horace Mann Junior High. The area was busy and crossing streets like 15th and Boston was dangerous enough that my mother didn't allow me a bicycle. J.E. Crosby's home was a landmark in my neighborhood.
My brother now lives on the family property near Siloam Springs, Arkansas. He is ninety-two years old and a widower. That area has become part of our family's extended home outside of Tulsa.

David Gettis was my great-grandfather on my grandmother's side from Scotland. After my grandmother married and moved to America, he sold his business interests back home and brought his family to Springfield, Missouri. He was part of the family's immigration story from Scotland to the U.S.
